title: A Guide to Payroll in Vietnam: What Employers Need to Know for 2026
metaDescription: A 2026 payroll guide for employers in Vietnam: minimum wage by region, the new PIT brackets, statutory insurance caps, overtime rules, e-SIB digital compliance, and the in-house versus outsourcing decision.

hero.title: A Guide to Payroll in Vietnam: What Employers Need to Know for 2026
hero.subtitle: The wage, tax, insurance, and overtime changes shaping payroll compliance in Vietnam this year, and how to manage them.

sections.heading: What's Changing with Payroll in Vietnam Right Now
body: Vietnam's 2026 payroll rules deliver a rare combination: several changes increase employees' net pay without raising base salaries, while tightening enforcement on employers. Understanding the mechanics is essential to staying compliant and competitive in the labor market.

Three shifts stand out this year. The personal income tax system has been simplified and made more generous, statutory insurance caps are rising mid-year, and payroll records have gone fully digital with real-time enforcement.

sections.heading: Minimum Wage and Tax System Overhaul
items: The 2026 minimum wage rose 7.2% across the four regions, ranging from VND 3.7 million to VND 5.31 million per month, and sets the baseline for overtime, severance, and probationary caps.
Personal income tax was simplified from seven brackets to five.
The personal allowance increased from VND 11 million to VND 15.5 million per month, plus VND 6.2 million per dependent.
Tech professionals can qualify for a five-year full PIT exemption or a 50% tax reduction, boosting recruitment appeal without expanding the payroll budget.

sections.heading: Statutory Insurance Contributions
body: Employers contribute 23.5% of gross salary toward statutory insurance, calculated on employee earnings up to a cap. That cap is increasing from VND 46.8 million to VND 50.54 million on 1 July 2026.

For high-earning workers, the higher cap adds roughly VND 878,000 per month in employer contributions. Budgeting for this mid-year step-up is important for companies with senior or specialist staff above the previous threshold.

sections.heading: Overtime and Shift Rules
items: Regular weekday overtime: 150% of the hourly wage
Night shifts (10 PM to 6 AM): an additional 30% premium
Annual overtime cap of 200 to 300 hours depending on the sector

sections.heading: Going Digital: How e-SIBs Are Changing Payroll
body: As of 1 January 2026, Electronic Social Insurance Books (e-SIBs) have fully replaced paper records. Government systems now monitor contributions in real time and automatically flag problems.

Late insurance payments accrue 0.03% daily interest, and underpayment violations can draw fines of up to VND 150 million plus required back-pay. With enforcement automated, accuracy and timeliness are no longer optional.

sections.heading: Should You Keep Payroll In-House or Outsource It?
body: The economics increasingly favor outsourcing. Running payroll in-house for around 20 employees typically costs VND 15 to 25 million per month, while outsourcing the same workload runs roughly VND 3 to 8 million.

Beyond cost, external providers absorb legal liability and automatically update their processes for regulatory changes, such as the mid-year insurance cap increase or the new PIT brackets. For many employers, that combination of lower cost and reduced risk is decisive.
